reading.title: The Rise of Co-working Spaces
reading.text:
Over the past decade, co-working spaces have become increasingly popular among freelancers, remote workers, and small businesses. Unlike traditional offices, co-working spaces allow people to rent a desk or room by the hour, day or month, giving workers the flexibility they need without long-term commitments.

For many workers, the social aspect is just as important as the practical benefits. Working alone at home can be isolating, and co-working spaces provide opportunities to network with professionals from different industries. Studies suggest that members of co-working spaces report higher levels of motivation compared to those who work from home full-time.

However, co-working spaces are not suitable for everyone. Jobs that require strict confidentiality, or that involve frequent phone calls, may not be compatible with a shared environment.

reading.title: Should Social Media Companies Be Held Responsible for Harmful Content?
reading.text:
The debate over whether social media platforms should bear legal responsibility for the content posted by their users has intensified in recent years. Proponents of greater regulation argue that the sheer scale of these platforms — some with over a billion active users — gives them an unprecedented ability to shape public opinion, and that this power must come with accountability.

Critics of this position, however, contend that holding platforms liable for user-generated content would have a chilling effect on free expression. If companies faced significant penalties for every post deemed harmful, they might adopt overly cautious moderation policies, effectively silencing legitimate speech.

A more nuanced approach might involve distinguishing between platforms that passively host content and those that actively promote it through algorithmic amplification. Platforms that deliberately push controversial content to maximise engagement could reasonably be held to a higher standard than those that merely store and display it.

reading.title: The Limits of Meritocracy
reading.text:
The idea that society should reward individuals based on talent and effort — what we call meritocracy — is so deeply embedded in modern thinking that questioning it can feel almost subversive. Yet a growing body of scholarship suggests that meritocracy, as it functions in practice, may perpetuate the very inequalities it claims to overcome.

The core problem is that the conditions for merit are not equally distributed. Access to high-quality education, social capital, and professional networks is profoundly shaped by the circumstances of one's birth. In this context, what appears to be the reward for individual achievement may, on closer inspection, be the compounding of inherited advantage.

Furthermore, Michael Sandel has argued that meritocracy breeds a particularly corrosive form of hubris among the successful — a belief that their achievements are entirely self-made — alongside a corresponding sense of humiliation among those who feel left behind. This dynamic, he suggests, has contributed to the political polarisation visible across many democracies.

None of this implies that we should abandon the aspiration towards a society in which effort is rewarded. Rather, it suggests the need for a more honest reckoning with the structural conditions that determine who gets to compete, and on what terms.

reading.title: The Interview
reading.text:
Maya had rehearsed her answers twenty times in front of the mirror. She had prepared for every question she could imagine — her strengths, her weaknesses, where she saw herself in five years. What she had not prepared for was arriving at the wrong address.

Standing outside a shuttered bakery in the rain, she checked her phone. The email clearly said Millbank House, but this was clearly Millbank Lane. She had ten minutes.

She ran. By the time she pushed open the glass doors of the correct building, her hair was damp and her shoes were squeaking on the marble floor. The receptionist looked up.

'I have an interview at two o'clock,' Maya said, slightly out of breath.

The receptionist smiled. 'Ms Reyes? They've moved it to the third floor. Oh — and the interviewer is running late too. You have plenty of time.'

reading.title: The Hidden Costs of Fast Fashion
reading.text:
Fast fashion has transformed clothing retail since the 1990s, enabling consumers to purchase trend-led garments at remarkably low prices. Yet beneath this affordability lies a complex web of environmental and human costs that brands have long been reluctant to make visible.

The fashion industry is responsible for approximately 10% of global carbon emissions — more than all international flights and maritime shipping combined. The dyeing and treatment of textiles is estimated to account for 20% of industrial water pollution. In Bangladesh, Cambodia, and Vietnam, where much production is concentrated, rivers running close to garment factories frequently carry toxic runoff into communities that depend on them for drinking water and agriculture.

The social dimension is equally troubling. While fast fashion companies promote a narrative of "democratising fashion," the workers producing these garments frequently earn wages that fall short of what researchers define as a living wage. Wage theft — the non-payment or underpayment of legally owed earnings — is endemic in global supply chains.

Crucially, the industry's opacity makes accountability difficult. Many brands maintain only superficial oversight of their supply chains, subcontracting to third parties who may themselves subcontract further. When disasters occur — as with the Rana Plaza factory collapse in 2013, which killed 1,134 workers — accountability dissipates across a labyrinth of corporate structures.

reading.title: The Case for a Four-Day Working Week
reading.text:
Proponents of the four-day working week argue that compressing the same workload into fewer days yields measurable benefits in employee wellbeing, productivity, and talent retention — without sacrificing organisational output.

The evidence from recent pilots is encouraging. A large-scale trial conducted across Iceland between 2015 and 2019 found that productivity remained the same or improved in the vast majority of workplaces tested, while reported burnout declined significantly. Microsoft Japan recorded a 40% jump in productivity after trialling a compressed schedule in 2019.

Opponents counter that such results are difficult to generalise. Many roles — in healthcare, logistics, and customer services — are structured around continuous service demands that cannot easily accommodate reduced scheduling. There is also a risk that the transition disproportionately burdens lower-paid workers, who are less likely to hold flexible roles and more likely to face intensified pressure to accomplish the same volume of work in reduced time.

A more nuanced view holds that rather than a uniform policy, the question is whether organisations have the autonomy to design working patterns that serve both business needs and human flourishing. The four-day week may be an effective solution for some, but it risks becoming a simplistic answer to a structurally complex question about the nature of work itself.

reading.title: An Unexpected Invitation
reading.text:
The envelope had been sitting on Mira's kitchen table for three days before she opened it. It bore no return address — only her name, in handwriting she recognised but couldn't quite place.

Inside was a single card: an invitation to a reunion of the physics department from her undergraduate years, to be held at a hotel in Edinburgh on the last Saturday of the month. At the bottom, in a different hand, someone had added: "I do hope you'll come. There is something I've been meaning to say."

Mira set the card down and stared at it for a long moment. She had not attended a departmental event in twelve years, not since the acrimonious end of the collaboration that had defined — and effectively concluded — the most promising phase of her research career. She had told herself that she had simply moved on. But the handwriting at the bottom nagged at her.

She picked up the card again and held it at arm's length. The script was slightly cramped and tilted rightward. It was, she was now quite certain, the handwriting of Professor Tallis — the man who had published her research without attribution and then denied it in front of the entire faculty.

She wrote RSVP: YES on a slip of paper, placed it in an envelope, and went to find a stamp.

reading.title: Urban Heat Islands: A Growing Challenge
reading.text:
As cities expand, they increasingly replace vegetation and natural terrain with concrete, asphalt, and glass — materials that absorb and radiate solar heat far more intensely than the surfaces they displace. The result is a well-documented meteorological phenomenon known as the urban heat island effect: cities consistently record higher temperatures than the rural areas surrounding them, sometimes by as much as 7°C at night.

The consequences are significant. Elevated urban temperatures increase energy consumption as residents rely on air conditioning, which in turn drives further heat generation. Heat-related illnesses and deaths are disproportionately concentrated in urban centres, with elderly populations and those in lower-income housing — which tends to lack adequate insulation — most acutely affected.

Planners and urban designers are exploring a range of interventions. Green roofs, which layer soil and vegetation over conventional roofing, have been shown to reduce surface temperatures by up to 30°C compared to standard rooftops. Tree canopy cover is equally important: research in Melbourne found that a 10% increase in urban tree cover was associated with a 3°C reduction in mean maximum temperature. Cool pavement technology, which uses reflective or permeable materials, is being trialled in cities from Los Angeles to Singapore.

Whether these measures can keep pace with both expanding urbanisation and the warming trend attributable to climate change remains, at best, uncertain.

reading.title: The Paradox of Choice in Consumer Culture
reading.text:
In 1956, the psychologist George Miller published what became one of the most cited papers in cognitive science, arguing that human working memory could hold only seven items, give or take two. Sixty years later, the implications of this observation have spread far beyond the laboratory and into every domain of modern consumer life.

Barry Schwartz, in his influential 2004 book, popularised the term "the paradox of choice" to describe a counterintuitive phenomenon: that beyond a certain threshold, the proliferation of options does not increase consumer satisfaction but actively diminishes it. Drawing on research in psychology, economics, and behavioural science, Schwartz argued that an excess of alternatives generates what he called "choice overload" — a combination of decision fatigue, opportunity cost anxiety, and post-purchase regret.

The evidence from controlled studies is instructive. A now-famous experiment by Iyengar and Lepper placed either 6 or 24 varieties of jam on display in a supermarket. Customers were far more likely to stop at the larger display; but they were ten times more likely to purchase jam from the smaller one. The abundance of options, it appeared, was stimulating but paralysing.

Critics of the paradox of choice thesis have noted that the phenomenon may be culturally bounded. Research conducted in cultures with collectivist values has yielded different results, with some studies finding that more choice correlates with higher satisfaction. Nevertheless, the broader point — that the equation of freedom with maximal consumer choice rests on shakier psychological grounds than mainstream economics has traditionally assumed — retains its force in Western contexts.

reading.title: The Cartographer's Dilemma
reading.text:
Every map is a lie — or so the cartographer's adage goes. To flatten a sphere onto a plane, something must be sacrificed: area, shape, distance, or direction. Gerardus Mercator understood this better than anyone. When he produced his famous projection in 1569, his primary purpose was practical: to allow navigators to plot straight-line courses across the open ocean. To achieve this, he systematically enlarged the landmasses of the Northern Hemisphere, a distortion that rendered Greenland approximately the size of Africa on paper, despite the continent being fourteen times larger in reality.

For five centuries, this representational choice went largely unremarked in the West. But in 1974, the German historian Arno Peters introduced an alternative projection claiming to represent the relative areas of all landmasses with mathematical accuracy. Predictably, it stirred controversy. Peters was accused by cartographers of overstating his claims and understating the technical problems with his own projection. But his challenge to Mercator also surfaced an uncomfortable question: if a projection that enlarges Europe and North America at the expense of Africa, South America, and Asia had been the default map of the world for five centuries, what subliminal effect might this have had on how populations in the North perceived — and still perceive — the relative significance of the global South?

The cartographic debate, in this light, is not merely a technical matter. Maps do not merely represent the world; they help to constitute our understanding of it. And in choosing how to distort — because distortion is always inevitable — map-makers necessarily make choices that are never purely neutral.

options: Mercator's projection is technically superior to Peters' projection
Maps are purely scientific instruments that should avoid distortion

Representational choices in maps carry cultural and political implications

options: To accurately show the relative sizes of all continents
To serve the navigational needs of seafarers
To represent Europe as the most important continent

That Peters' projection would have prevented colonial exploitation

reading.title: The Psychology of Conformity Revisited
reading.text:
Solomon Asch's conformity experiments of the early 1950s remain among the most replicated and debated findings in all of social psychology. In the original study, participants were asked to judge which of three lines matched a standard line in length — a task so simple that errors in isolation were virtually non-existent. Yet when surrounded by confederates who unanimously gave the wrong answer, approximately 75% of participants conformed to the incorrect response on at least one occasion.

The findings have frequently been interpreted as demonstrating the disturbing ease with which social pressure can override even straightforward perceptual judgement. But more recent scholarship has complicated this reading. Neuro-imaging studies conducted since the 1990s suggest that for some participants, the experience of disagreeing with the group produced genuine distortions in perceptual processing — not merely compliance with group norms, but an actual change in what the participant perceived. The discomfort of dissent, it appears, can operate at a more fundamental cognitive level than was previously assumed.

A second complication is cultural. Cross-cultural replications of Asch's paradigm have yielded significantly higher conformity rates in societies characterised by collectivist values, suggesting that the degree of social influence is modulated by cultural context. What Asch documented may have been a psychological mechanism that is universal in its existence but highly variable in its intensity.

These refinements do not diminish the importance of Asch's original insight — that humans are social animals whose judgements are patterned in ways that rational-choice models struggle to account for. They do, however, caution against overly deterministic conclusions about the human capacity for independent thought.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of this text?
options: To argue that Asch's experiments were methodologically flawed
To present Asch's findings and explore more recent refinements to them

reading.title: Rewilding: Promise and Provocation
reading.text:
The concept of rewilding — the large-scale restoration of ecosystems by reintroducing species that once played a functional role within them — has moved from the margins of conservation biology to its mainstream with remarkable speed. Where once the field was preoccupied with preserving existing habitats and species in situ, a growing body of researchers now argues that passive conservation is insufficient in the face of what many describe as a sixth mass extinction event.

The ecological rationale for rewilding rests on the concept of trophic cascades — the way in which apex predators, by regulating prey populations, prevent overgrazing and thereby protect plant communities, soil stability, and downstream water quality. The reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone National Park in 1995 became a frequently cited case study: within years, the altered behaviour of elk herds, which no longer grazed as heavily on riverbanks, allowed vegetation to recover, riverbanks to stabilise, and fish and beaver populations to increase. The rivers themselves, some researchers argued, began to change course.

Critics of rewilding have raised legitimate concerns. In densely populated regions of Europe, the reintroduction of large predators provokes inevitable conflicts with livestock farming and rural livelihoods. There is also a scientific counterargument: that the ecosystems being "restored" are not static historical states but dynamic, evolving systems, and that selecting one historical baseline as a restoration target involves a degree of arbitrariness that the scientific community has yet to fully resolve.

Proponents counter that the alternative — continued fragmentation and species loss — is no alternative at all.

reading.title: Digital Detox
reading.context: Read this article from a student magazine.
reading.text: Last summer, journalist and blogger Sara Chen challenged herself to spend one week without her smartphone. She expected boredom. What she did not expect was how much she noticed about the world around her. 'On the first day, I kept reaching for my pocket,' she wrote. 'By the third day, I had started talking to strangers on buses.' Sara observed that without the constant pull of notifications, she felt less anxious but also less informed. 'I missed real news but not the endless scroll of social media,' she admitted. She completed the week but did not make the change permanent. 'Total disconnection isn't the answer for most people,' she concluded. 'But even reducing screen time by 20 percent can make a real difference to your wellbeing.' Her article was widely shared — ironically, mostly on social media platforms.

questions.question: What surprised Sara most about her digital detox?
options: She became more observant of her surroundings
She missed her social media more than she expected
She found it easy to stop using her phone

options: A technical error in social media apps
The habit of continuously browsing social media
A specific type of social media content
A new feature on smartphone screens

questions.question: What was Sara's final conclusion about digital detox?
options: Everyone should give up smartphones permanently
Reducing screen time moderately is more realistic than total disconnection

Social media should be avoided completely

questions.question: Why does the writer call it 'ironic' that Sara's article was shared on social media?
options: Sara wrote the article to criticise social media, yet people used it to share her work
Sara had never used social media before writing the article
The article was too long to be read on a mobile screen
Social media platforms paid Sara to write the article

reading.title: Working the Night Shift
reading.context: Read this email from a student to her tutor.
reading.text:
Subject: Request to Change Seminar Group

Dear Dr Patterson,

I am writing to ask whether it might be possible to transfer to the Thursday morning seminar group for your Critical Thinking module.

I am currently in the Tuesday evening group, which runs from 6pm to 8pm. However, I recently accepted a part-time job at a local hotel, where I work Tuesday and Wednesday evenings. I took the job to help cover my living costs this year. I realise I should have checked for scheduling conflicts before accepting the position, and I apologise for not doing so.

I have already spoken to two students in the Thursday group who have agreed to swap with me if you give your permission. I understand if this is not possible due to group size limits.

I have not missed any sessions so far and have submitted all assignments on time. I hope this demonstrates my commitment to the course.

Thank you for considering my request.

Best regards,
Amelia Johansson

questions.question: Why does Amelia want to change seminar groups?
options: She dislikes the other students in her current group
Her part-time work hours conflict with her current seminar time
She finds the Tuesday group too large
Her current tutor has been changed

options: She is admitting she made a mistake when accepting the job
She is asking the tutor to check the timetable for her
She is explaining that the university made a scheduling error
She is criticising the company she works for

options: She has found other students willing to swap places with her
She has already attended the Thursday group unofficially
She has asked the university administration for approval
She has reduced her working hours at the hotel

To prove she is a responsible and committed student

reading.title: Urban Farming
reading.context: Read this article about a growing trend in cities.
reading.text: On the rooftop of a former factory in Berlin, tomatoes, courgettes and herbs grow in long rows of soil-filled containers. This is not a traditional farm — it sits eight floors above street level, surrounded by office buildings. Urban farming, once considered a novelty, is rapidly becoming a serious part of how some cities approach food production. The Berlin rooftop project supplies produce to three nearby restaurants and sells the rest directly to local residents. Its founders argue that growing food in cities reduces the distance it travels, cutting carbon emissions and improving freshness. Critics point out that rooftop farms are expensive to set up and can only produce a small fraction of what a city needs. However, supporters believe the real value lies not just in food production but in community building and education. 'When people grow food themselves, they think differently about what they eat,' says project coordinator Marta Hofmann. Several major supermarket chains have begun to invest in the concept, suggesting that urban farming may soon move from the margins to the mainstream.

questions.question: Where does the Berlin rooftop farm send its produce?
options: To nearby restaurants and local residents

questions.question: What environmental benefit does the article mention?
options: Urban farms use rainwater instead of tap water
Food travels shorter distances, reducing carbon emissions
Rooftop plants help to cool the city
Urban farms eliminate the need for pesticides

questions.question: What does Marta Hofmann suggest is an important benefit beyond food production?
options: Urban farming creates full-time employment for city workers
Growing food changes people's relationship with what they eat
Rooftop farms are more profitable than traditional agriculture
Urban farming reduces the cost of food for low-income families

questions.question: What does the involvement of supermarket chains suggest about urban farming?
options: Supermarkets are trying to put urban farmers out of business
Urban farming is likely to become more widespread and commercially significant
The concept is too expensive for individual consumers to support
Urban farms will soon replace all traditional forms of agriculture

reading.title: The Gig Economy and Worker Rights
reading.context: Read this extract from a business and society magazine.
reading.text: The proliferation of gig economy platforms — from food delivery apps to freelance marketplaces — has fundamentally altered the relationship between labour and capital. Proponents argue that such platforms democratise opportunity: anyone with a vehicle, a skill, or a spare room can generate income. Yet critics contend that the flexibility prized by platforms often masks a precariousness that traditional employment protections were designed to prevent. Workers classified as 'independent contractors' typically receive no sick pay, no pension contributions, and no guaranteed minimum income. Several European courts have begun reclassifying gig workers as employees, reasoning that the degree of control platforms exercise over their workers — dictating prices, assigning tasks algorithmically, and penalising poor ratings — is incompatible with genuine self-employment. Platform companies respond that reclassification would destroy the flexibility that both workers and consumers value, and that many gig workers actively prefer autonomy over security. The debate reflects a deeper tension: economies built on flexibility may generate growth and convenience, but may simultaneously erode the social contracts that post-war welfare states were constructed to protect.

questions.question: What is the central tension described in this article?
options: The conflict between technology companies and government regulators
The balance between economic flexibility and worker protection
The difference between skilled and unskilled gig economy workers
The competition between domestic and international gig platforms

questions.question: What does the word 'precariousness' most nearly mean in this context?
options: A high level of physical danger

questions.question: On what basis have some courts reclassified gig workers as employees?
options: Because gig workers earn less than the national minimum wage
Because platforms exert a level of control inconsistent with independent work
Because gig platforms have failed to pay taxes correctly
Because gig workers have formally applied for employee status

questions.question: What does the phrase 'social contracts that post-war welfare states were constructed to protect' refer to?
options: International trade agreements signed after World War II
Legal contracts between employers and unions
The implicit societal guarantees of security, healthcare, and fair pay
The constitutional rights of workers in democratic nations

reading.title: Rewilding: Restoring What Was Lost
reading.context: Read this article from an environmental science publication.
reading.text: Rewilding — the large-scale restoration of ecosystems to a more natural state — has moved from the fringes of conservation biology to a prominent position in global biodiversity policy. Its most celebrated projects have involved the reintroduction of apex predators: wolves in Yellowstone, lynx in several European nations, and, most controversially, plans to return bears to parts of the Scottish Highlands. Proponents argue that the absence of large predators creates 'trophic cascades' — chain reactions that degrade entire ecosystems. When wolves were reintroduced to Yellowstone in 1995, deer populations were redistributed, allowing vegetation to recover along riverbanks, which in turn stabilised soils and altered the course of rivers. Critics, however, question whether ecosystems degraded over centuries can simply be restored, and raise legitimate concerns about the safety of rural communities and livestock. The deeper philosophical question is whether rewilding represents a return to a more authentic natural state, or an act of human hubris — attempting to reverse consequences that may themselves have become 'natural' through the passage of time.

questions.question: What does 'trophic cascades' mean as described in the article?
options: Rapid population growth in apex predator species
Chain reactions through an ecosystem triggered by changes at the top of the food chain
The migration of predators across national boundaries
A technique for measuring biodiversity in restored habitats

questions.question: Which specific effect did wolf reintroduction have at Yellowstone?
options: It reduced the wolf population in neighbouring states
It caused deer populations to decline significantly
It allowed riverbank vegetation to recover, stabilising soils
It attracted significant numbers of tourists to the park

questions.question: What concern do critics of rewilding raise beyond the philosophical question?
options: Rewilding projects are too expensive to be sustainable
Reintroduced species may spread disease to native wildlife
Rural communities and their livestock may face risks from large predators
Rewilding may damage the tourism industry

questions.question: The phrase 'act of human hubris' suggests that some critics view rewilding as what?
options: An arrogant overestimation of humanity's ability to control nature
A politically motivated attempt to undermine farming communities
An impractical idea supported only by urban environmentalists
A misuse of scientific research for commercial purposes

reading.title: Third Culture Kids
reading.context: Read this article about identity and upbringing.
reading.text: Sociologist Ruth Hill Useem coined the term 'Third Culture Kids' (TCKs) in the 1950s to describe children who spend significant portions of their developmental years outside their parents' home culture. The 'third culture' refers not to any specific nationality, but to the hybrid identity that forms when elements of the home culture and the host culture are synthesised. Today, the global mobility of corporations, diplomatic services, and international organisations means that the TCK population is larger and more diverse than ever. Research consistently finds that TCKs tend to develop strong intercultural communication skills and demonstrate high levels of adaptability. However, they also face particular challenges: a sense of not fully belonging to any single culture, difficulty maintaining long-term friendships, and what some psychologists describe as a 'hidden grief' — the repeated loss of community with each relocation. As adults, many TCKs find that their most natural social environment is international or multicultural spaces, where their fluid sense of identity is an asset rather than an anomaly.

questions.question: According to the article, what does the 'third culture' describe?
options: A specific culture formed in a third country
A blend of home and host cultures that creates a new hybrid identity
The culture of international organisations like the United Nations
The experience of being a third-generation immigrant

questions.question: Which challenge specific to TCKs does the article describe as a 'hidden grief'?
options: Struggling to learn new languages in each new country
Losing community and friendships repeatedly with each move
Feeling pressure to perform academically in multiple school systems
Experiencing discrimination as a foreigner in the host country

questions.question: Why might TCKs find multicultural spaces more comfortable as adults?
options: Because they are more likely to be employed by international organisations
Because their mixed identity is normal and valued rather than unusual
Because they prefer speaking foreign languages to their mother tongue
Because multicultural cities offer better career opportunities

questions.question: What does 'fluid' mean in the phrase 'fluid sense of identity'?
options: Flexible and not fixed to a single definition

reading.title: The Science of Sleep Debt
reading.context: Read this extract from a popular science magazine.
reading.text: The idea that lost sleep can simply be recovered at the weekend — 'sleep banking' — has gained widespread currency, particularly among students and shift workers. Recent research, however, suggests this folk remedy may be dangerously misleading. A 2019 study published in Current Biology found that while weekend recovery sleep does reduce feelings of sleepiness, it does not fully restore metabolic function, attention, or reaction times. Participants who slept for six hours on weekdays and recovered at weekends still showed significantly impaired performance on cognitive tasks compared to those who maintained consistent sleep schedules. More concerning still, chronic sleep restriction appears to alter brain physiology in ways that are not reversed by short-term recovery. 'Sleep debt is not like financial debt,' explains neuroscientist Dr Aarav Singh. 'You cannot simply pay it back with interest on a Saturday morning.' The practical implications extend beyond personal wellbeing: industries with high rates of sleep-deprived workers — transport, medicine, emergency services — face elevated safety risks that weekend recovery sleep cannot adequately mitigate.

questions.question: What is the main argument of this article?
options: People should sleep earlier in the evening to avoid sleep debt
Weekend recovery sleep does not fully compensate for weekday sleep deprivation
Shift workers are the main victims of sleep-related health problems
Six hours of sleep per night is sufficient for most adults

questions.question: According to the 2019 study, what remained impaired after weekend recovery sleep?
options: Metabolic function, attention, and reaction times

questions.question: What does 'mitigate' mean in the final sentence?
options: Reduce or lessen the severity of

questions.question: What does Dr Singh's analogy about financial debt imply?
options: Sleep should be treated as a form of economic investment
Sleeping more at weekends makes the problem worse, not better
It is impossible to recover from chronic sleep deprivation at all
Short-term recovery sleep does not undo the accumulated physiological damage

reading.title: Attention and Architecture
reading.context: Read this article about the design of learning environments.
reading.text: Researchers at the University of Salford published a landmark study in 2015 examining the relationship between classroom design and student attainment. Over the course of a year, they tracked 3,766 pupils across 153 classrooms and found that physical environment accounted for up to 16 percent of the variation in academic progress. Key factors included natural light, air quality, temperature, and the flexibility of the learning space. Schools with large windows, controllable ventilation, and adaptable furniture layouts produced measurably better outcomes than those with fixed rows of desks under artificial lighting. The findings have since influenced the design briefs of educational authorities in several countries. Yet the implementation of these principles faces a structural obstacle: the majority of school buildings in the developed world were constructed during a period — typically the mid-twentieth century — when such considerations were either unknown or considered a luxury. Retrofitting existing buildings is expensive, and with education budgets under pressure globally, the gap between what research recommends and what policy can fund remains stubbornly wide.

questions.question: What percentage of variation in academic progress was linked to classroom design?
Which physical features were associated with better outcomes?
options: New technology, including interactive whiteboards and tablets
Natural light, air quality, temperature, and flexible furniture
Small class sizes and colourful wall displays
Proximity to green spaces and outdoor learning areas

questions.question: Why does the article say implementation faces a 'structural obstacle'?
options: Teachers resist changes to their working environment
School construction rules prevent alterations to existing buildings
Most school buildings predate the research and are expensive to modify
The research findings have not yet been replicated in other countries

questions.question: What does 'stubbornly' suggest about the gap between research and policy?
options: The gap is narrowing but slowly
The gap has remained difficult to close despite awareness of the problem
The gap is caused by deliberate political decisions
The gap is primarily a problem in developing countries

reading.title: The Paradox of Choice
reading.context: Read this extract from a behavioural economics essay.
reading.text: Conventional economic theory holds that more choice is invariably preferable — a greater number of options expands individual autonomy and increases the probability of finding an optimal match for one's preferences. Psychologist Barry Schwartz challenged this orthodoxy systematically in his 2004 work, arguing that beyond a certain threshold, the proliferation of options produces not satisfaction but paralysis and regret. The mechanism is twofold. First, evaluating a larger number of alternatives generates greater cognitive load, increasing the likelihood of decision avoidance or arbitrary choice. Second, the selection of one option necessitates the rejection of others, and the more appealing those rejected alternatives, the greater the 'opportunity cost' experienced as post-decision regret. Schwartz distinguishes between 'maximisers' — those who seek the objectively best option — and 'satisficers', who seek a 'good enough' solution. Counterintuitively, his research suggests that satisficers report higher levels of subjective wellbeing, precisely because they are insulated from the relentless comparative logic that maximising entails. The practical implications for product design, public policy, and education are considerable: simplifying choice architectures, rather than expanding them, may better serve human flourishing.

questions.question: Which statement best summarises Schwartz's central argument?
options: Consumer choice should be regulated by governments to prevent exploitation
Economic theory underestimates the importance of individual preferences
An excess of options can reduce wellbeing rather than enhance it
Satisficers are less intelligent than maximisers in their decision-making

questions.question: In this context, what does 'opportunity cost' refer to?
options: The financial cost of changing one's decision after the fact
The psychological pain of forgoing attractive alternatives when making a choice
The time wasted in evaluating too many options
The commercial value of an item that was not purchased

questions.question: Why does Schwartz find it 'counterintuitive' that satisficers report higher wellbeing?
options: Because satisficers earn less money than maximisers on average
Because conventional wisdom assumes that seeking the best outcome leads to greater happiness
Because satisficers make decisions more quickly and with less information
Because the research was conducted before behavioural economics was established

questions.question: What does the phrase 'relentless comparative logic' describe?
options: The formal methods used in economic modelling
The habit of continuously measuring one's choices against all available alternatives
A philosophical tradition in European thought
The psychological tendency to prefer familiar options

reading.title: Linguistic Relativity Revisited
reading.context: Read this extract from a cognitive science journal.
reading.text: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis — or the principle of linguistic relativity — proposes that the language one speaks shapes, to some degree, the cognitive categories available for perceiving and organising experience. In its strong form, largely discredited by mid-twentieth century linguistics, language was held to determine thought entirely. The weak form, however, has received renewed empirical support in recent decades. Studies of spatial reasoning have found that speakers of languages using absolute directions (north, south) rather than relative ones (left, right) demonstrate superior navigational performance, suggesting that habitual linguistic practice cultivates corresponding cognitive capacities. Similarly, research on colour perception indicates that language communities with distinct lexical terms for shades that other languages group together show faster discrimination of those shades under time pressure. Critics caution against overstating these effects: the influence of language on thought appears to be probabilistic and domain-specific rather than deterministic. Nevertheless, these findings carry significant implications for second language acquisition, translation theory, and the emerging field of computational linguistics, where the assumption that all languages encode the same conceptual universe has been called increasingly into question.

questions.question: What distinguishes the 'strong form' from the 'weak form' of the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis?
options: The strong form applies only to non-European languages
The strong form claims language determines thought entirely; the weak form claims partial influence
The weak form was proposed by Sapir and the strong form by Whorf
The strong form has been confirmed by recent neuroscience research

questions.question: What do studies of spatial reasoning suggest about speakers of absolute-direction languages?
options: They struggle with abstract reasoning tasks
They are more likely to become professional geographers
They learn foreign languages more easily

questions.question: What does 'probabilistic and domain-specific' imply about language's influence on thought?
options: The effect is mathematically predictable and applies universally
The effect is likely in certain areas but not absolute or universal
The effect has only been observed in laboratory conditions
The effect is a statistical error caused by cultural bias

questions.question: Why might the article's findings challenge assumptions in computational linguistics?
options: They suggest that machine translation is inherently impossible
They imply that languages do not map onto a single shared conceptual system, complicating translation between them
They demonstrate that computers cannot learn language the way humans do
They show that linguistic relativity is more significant than grammatical structure

reading.title: The Attention Economy
reading.context: Read this extract from an essay on digital media and society.
reading.text: The metaphor that best captures contemporary digital capitalism is not the factory but the theatre — a space engineered not to produce goods but to capture and hold the gaze. The concept of the attention economy, developed most fully by psychologist and Nobel laureate Herbert Simon and later popularised by economist Michael Goldhaber, rests on a deceptively simple inversion of traditional scarcity logic: in an age of information abundance, it is not information but the human capacity to attend to it that constitutes the scarce resource. Platforms designed under this logic are not passive conduits for content but active architects of engagement, deploying variable reward mechanisms borrowed from behavioural psychology to maintain compulsive use. The consequences extend well beyond individual screen time: when attention becomes the currency through which platforms generate revenue, the incentives structurally favour content that provokes strong emotional responses — outrage, anxiety, envy — over content that informs, edifies, or promotes deliberation. Some theorists argue that this architecture represents not merely a market inefficiency but a threat to the epistemic conditions necessary for democratic governance, which depends on citizens capable of sustained, reflective attention.

questions.question: What is the central claim of the attention economy model as described here?
options: Digital platforms earn revenue primarily through advertising to wealthy consumers
In an information-rich environment, human attention — not information — becomes the scarce commodity
Social media companies deliberately create addictive products to harm users
The decline of traditional manufacturing has been replaced by digital services

questions.question: What does the writer mean by 'variable reward mechanisms borrowed from behavioural psychology'?
options: Loyalty programmes that offer discounts for frequent platform use
Techniques that trigger compulsive engagement by providing unpredictable rewards
Personalised content recommendations based on user data
Psychological tests used in platform design research

questions.question: Why does the attention economy structurally favour emotionally provocative content?
options: Users prefer emotional content to factual reporting
Emotional content is cheaper to produce than investigative journalism
Emotional content holds attention more effectively, which is what generates platform revenue
Advertisers specifically request emotional content for their campaigns

questions.question: What does 'epistemic conditions' refer to in the final sentence?
options: The physical infrastructure necessary for internet access
The political institutions through which laws are made
The conditions related to how people form knowledge and reasoned beliefs
The economic circumstances that influence media ownership

reading.title: Automation and the Future of Work
reading.context: Read this extract from an economic policy report.
reading.text: Forecasts of technological unemployment have periodically gripped public imagination since the Luddite movement of the early nineteenth century, and have been periodically confounded by subsequent waves of labour absorption into new industries. The current moment is distinguished, however, by several features that give pause even to historically minded optimists. First, the pace of automation is unprecedented: tasks that economists had long assumed required distinctively human capacities — nuanced language processing, pattern recognition in complex data, legal and medical reasoning — have proved tractable to machine learning at a speed few anticipated. Second, and more critically, the benefits of automation appear to accrue disproportionately to capital holders and to a narrow class of highly skilled workers, while the costs — in the form of labour market disruption — fall on workers who lack the time, resources, or institutional support necessary to retrain. The distributional asymmetry this creates is not merely an economic problem; it is a political one, as it risks generating populations whose material circumstances render them susceptible to authoritarian mobilisation. The question facing policymakers is therefore not whether automation will transform labour markets — it already is — but how societies might be constituted to distribute its benefits equitably.

questions.question: Why does the author say 'historically minded optimists' have reason to 'pause' now?
options: Because previous predictions about automation proved completely wrong
Because current automation is faster and affects a broader range of skills than before
Because the Luddite movement was ultimately successful in halting industrialisation
Because historical evidence suggests automation always leads to unemployment

questions.question: What does 'distributional asymmetry' mean in this context?
options: An unequal geographic spread of automation across different countries
The uneven distribution of automation's benefits and costs across society
The technical imbalance between human and machine capabilities
A statistical discrepancy in labour market data

questions.question: What political risk does the author associate with automation-driven inequality?
options: Trade unions may gain excessive power in affected industries
Economically displaced populations may become susceptible to authoritarian political movements
Governments may lose tax revenue needed to fund retraining programmes
International competition for technology workers may destabilise economies

questions.question: What does the author identify as the key question for policymakers?
options: Whether to invest in automation or to protect traditional jobs
How to retrain workers who have been displaced by specific technologies
How societies can be structured to distribute the benefits of automation fairly
Whether artificial intelligence will eventually surpass human intelligence

reading.title: Ethical Dimensions of Algorithmic Decision-Making
reading.context: Read this extract from a technology ethics paper.
reading.text: The delegation of consequential decisions to algorithmic systems — in areas ranging from credit scoring and judicial risk assessment to medical diagnosis and university admissions — raises ethical questions that neither computer science nor law has yet satisfactorily resolved. At the centre of these questions is the problem of opacity: many high-performing machine learning models operate as 'black boxes', producing outputs that cannot be explained even by their designers in terms that would satisfy the transparency requirements of natural justice. A loan applicant denied credit, or a defendant assigned a high recidivism risk score, may have a legitimate interest in understanding why — an interest that serves not only their individual rights but the broader social function of accountability. A second problem concerns the replication of historical bias: models trained on historical data will, absent deliberate intervention, reproduce the patterns embedded in that data, including patterns that reflect past discrimination. Correcting for this bias is technically possible but involves value judgements about which forms of equality to prioritise — procedural, substantive, or demographic — that are irreducibly political rather than technical. The governance challenge, then, is to develop institutional frameworks capable of holding algorithmic systems to standards of accountability that their opacity makes it genuinely difficult to enforce.

questions.question: What does 'opacity' mean in the context of algorithmic systems?
options: The tendency of algorithms to produce inconsistent results
The inability to explain how a system arrives at its decisions
The deliberate concealment of data by technology companies
The commercial secrecy that surrounds proprietary systems

questions.question: Why might an individual have a legitimate interest in understanding an algorithmic decision?
options: To enable them to appeal the decision and influence future algorithms
Because transparency serves both individual rights and broader social accountability
Because complex decisions should always be made by human professionals
Because algorithms cannot legally make decisions about individuals

questions.question: Why does correcting algorithmic bias involve 'irreducibly political' judgements?
options: Because governments must approve any changes to commercially used algorithms
Because different definitions of equality reflect competing social values that cannot be resolved technically
Because the political history of discrimination cannot be accurately encoded in data
Because algorithmic bias is a result of deliberate political interference in technology

questions.question: What paradox does the final sentence identify?
options: Algorithmic systems are simultaneously too simple and too expensive to regulate
Society needs accountability from systems that are inherently difficult to hold accountable due to their opacity
Technology companies are too powerful to be regulated effectively by democratic governments
The most accurate algorithms are always the least transparent to their developers

Well, I worked at a retail store for two years during university. I dealt with customer complaints and processed returns.

turns.text: I appreciate you making time for this, David.

It showed. That said, there are a couple of areas I'd like us to focus on. Time management has been flagged a few times — particularly around the project scheduling system.
I know. I've found it difficult to adapt to the new software, honestly.
That's fair. We should have provided better onboarding for the tool. I'll arrange some additional training.
I'd really appreciate that.
The other thing — and this is more about perception than performance — is visibility in team meetings. You have good ideas, David, but you tend to hold back.
I suppose I do. I find it hard to break into the conversation when it moves quickly.

Yes, that would make a real difference.
Good. Let's review again in six weeks.

That's a strong foundation for either path, honestly. But with your field experience, the International Development programme might be the more natural fit. It has a strong practitioner focus — a lot of the case studies are drawn from Africa and South Asia.

Both programmes require a dissertation. But International Development also has an option to do a field placement in your second semester — it's competitive, but given your background you'd be well-placed to apply.
That's very helpful. What about employment outcomes?
International Development graduates tend to move into the development sector, multilaterals — UN agencies, that kind of thing. Public Policy is more oriented towards government and the civil service.
I think that confirms it for me. The development sector is where I see my career going.
In that case, I'd recommend getting your application in early — places fill up faster than you'd expect.

listening.title: A Radio Commentary on Hybrid Working
listening.taskContext: Listen to a radio commentary about changes in working patterns.

turns.text: What we're seeing now isn't simply a trend — it's a fundamental restructuring of how knowledge work gets done.
For the past three years, organisations have been running a largely unplanned experiment in hybrid working, and the data is starting to come in.
The results are mixed, but a few things are becoming clear.
First, productivity in roles that require deep, independent concentration has generally held up well — in some cases, improved.
But collaboration, creativity, and the informal mentoring of junior staff all suffer when people aren't physically present together.
There's also the question of equity. Hybrid policies that sound neutral on paper can entrench existing inequalities.
Workers with cramped home environments, caring responsibilities, or less reliable technology are at a structural disadvantage.
Meanwhile, those who can work from comfortable home offices accumulate the intangible benefits of being out of commute and having greater schedule flexibility.
The real challenge for organisations isn't figuring out how many days people should come in.

It's building the intentionality to make the days they're together genuinely productive — and making sure that flexibility doesn't become a mechanism that quietly widens the gap between those who have it and those who don't.

What does the speaker say is the real question organisations face?
options: How to monitor staff from home
How to design work environments for different task types
Whether to return to the office full-time

options: It is a permanent fundamental restructuring of knowledge work

listening.title: A Research Ethics Committee Discussion
listening.taskContext: Listen to a discussion at a university research ethics committee.

turns.text: So we have three proposals to consider today. I'd like to start with the Johnson study — the one on decision-making under financial stress.

Yes. My concern is with the deception element. Participants are told they're in a study about general problem-solving, when in fact the researchers are observing responses to simulated financial loss.

That's a legitimate concern, but deception in this context has substantial precedent. The key question is whether the deception is proportionate to the research value — and whether a full debrief is in place.
The protocol does include a debrief, yes. But I remain uneasy. Some participants may be in genuine financial distress. Simulating further loss, even experimentally, could cause harm.
That's precisely why the screening questionnaire matters. The protocol excludes anyone currently facing serious financial difficulty.
Does it? I thought the exclusion criterion was bankruptcy within the previous two years, which is a fairly narrow band.
That's a fair reading. I think we need the researchers to tighten that criterion before we can approve this.
Agreed. What about the consent forms? Are they plain language?
They've been revised. The readability score is now at a level appropriate for the general public.
Good. Can we move to conditional approval, subject to the exclusion criteria revision?
I'm comfortable with that, with a request for resubmission within thirty days.

turns.text: I've reviewed the heads of terms and there are a few points I'd like to revisit before we proceed.

listening.title: A Lecture Extract: Behavioural Economics
listening.taskContext: Listen to an extract from a university lecture on behavioural economics.

turns.text: I want to spend the remaining time today on one of the most counterintuitive findings in all of behavioural economics — what Thaler and Sunstein termed the default effect.

The insight is deceptively simple: people tend to accept whatever option is presented to them as the default, not because they've considered it carefully and found it optimal, but because changing course requires effort, and most of us are cognitively disposed to avoid effort when we can.
The classic demonstration involves organ donation consent rates across countries.
In countries that use an opt-in system — where citizens must actively register to donate — consent rates cluster around 15 to 30 percent.
In countries with opt-out systems — where you are presumed to consent unless you actively withdraw — rates reach 85 to 95 percent.
The decision being made is identical. The population is comparable. The only variable is which option requires action.
Now, some critics argue this is manipulation — that governments are essentially exploiting cognitive laziness to achieve a policy outcome.
Proponents — and Thaler and Sunstein coined the term "libertarian paternalism" for this approach — argue that defaults are unavoidable.
Every system has a default, even if it's inaction.
The question isn't whether to have a default, but whose interests it should serve.
And I'd invite you to hold that question in mind as we move on to examine nudge policy in public health.

What is the 'default effect'?
options: People always choose the most popular option
People tend to go with the pre-set option rather than actively choosing

questions.question: What policy implication does the speaker draw from the default effect?
options: People should be forced to make active choices
Policymakers can guide behaviour through choice architecture without removing freedom
Financial incentives are the best policy tool
Education campaigns are more effective than defaults

listening.title: The Listening Survey

script.speaker: Researcher
script.text: Our study asked whether professionals believe they are effective listeners, and whether colleagues agree. Eighty-three percent of four hundred managers rated themselves above average. We call this interpretive listening — a trainable skill, not an innate one.

listening.title: Urban Wildlife
listening.context: A narrator describes wildlife adapting to cities.

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: As cities expand, they encroach on habitats wildlife has occupied for millennia. Yet ecologists have observed something more nuanced than simple displacement. Urban environments are increasingly colonised by species that have proved capable of adapting to human-dominated landscapes. In London, peregrine falcons nest on skyscrapers. In Tokyo, raccoon dogs have established territories in suburban parks. In Berlin, wild boars root through residential gardens. What these examples share is not merely animals tolerating humans but actively exploiting the novel resources cities provide — artificial heat, food waste, and fewer large predators. This adaptation is not without cost. Urban animals show elevated stress hormones, disrupted sleep cycles from artificial light, and altered social behaviour. The city, it seems, is both refuge and trap.

listening.title: The Neuroscience of Memory
listening.context: A university lecture on memory and cognition.

script.speaker: Professor
script.text: Memory has long been compared to a filing cabinet — a stable repository from which accurate records can be retrieved. This analogy is profoundly misleading. Memory is not stored but reconstructed. Every time we recall an event, we rebuild it from fragmented traces, and in doing so we inevitably modify it. This phenomenon — reconsolidation — means the act of remembering is simultaneously an act of rewriting. Elizabeth Loftus demonstrated that participants shown subtly altered photographs of events they witnessed would, within days, confidently assert the altered version was what they saw. The legal implications were substantial: eyewitness testimony, long the gold standard of courtroom evidence, is systematically unreliable in ways neither witnesses nor juries appreciate. The practical implication is more philosophical: the life narratives we construct are creative acts as much as historical ones, shaped by who we are now as much as by what actually occurred.

questions.question: What is the central claim of this lecture?
options: Neuroscience proves human memory is less reliable than believed
Memory functions as a stable archive
Memory is reconstructive, meaning recall modifies the memory itself
Loftus proved witnesses are motivated to distort memories

questions.question: What does reconsolidation mean in this context?
options: The phenomenon by which memories are rewritten each time they are recalled
Consolidating multiple memories into one narrative
The gradual weakening of memories over time

questions.question: What philosophical implication does the professor draw?
options: We should not trust our memories for decisions
Our life narratives are partly shaped by who we are now, not purely by past events
Therapy is the best way to recover accurate memories

listening.title: Decolonising the Curriculum
listening.context: A professor explains decolonising education at an academic conference.

script.speaker: Moderator
script.text: Professor Adeyemi, the term decolonising the curriculum has provoked sharply divergent responses. What does it mean in practice?

script.speaker: Professor
script.text: At its most basic, it means examining who has historically been included in and excluded from educational canons — and why. It is not about removing texts, as critics assert, but about asking questions previously considered settled: whose knowledge counts, through whose lens has it been presented, and what has been systematically omitted. When a medical curriculum was designed around data from white male populations, that has real clinical consequences for diagnosing other groups. When a literature course presents European modernism as the apex of literary achievement, it positions non-Western traditions as peripheral. Decolonising means making those assumptions visible, questioning their legitimacy, and constructing syllabi that better reflect the global production of knowledge.

script.speaker: Moderator
script.text: Some argue this is politically motivated rather than academically driven.

script.speaker: Professor
script.text: The original curriculum was also politically motivated — it just was not perceived as such because it aligned with the assumptions of those who designed it. All knowledge systems are situated. The question is whether we are willing to acknowledge that.

questions.question: How does Professor Adeyemi define decolonising the curriculum?
options: Removing European texts from university reading lists
Examining whose knowledge has been included and excluded, questioning established assumptions
Replacing Western traditions with non-Western alternatives

questions.question: What medical example illustrates the consequences of a limited curriculum?
options: Underdiagnosis of mental health conditions in minorities
Medical training based primarily on white male data, affecting diagnosis of other groups
Overrepresentation of experimental treatments in research

questions.question: How does the professor respond to the accusation of political motivation?
options: Academic knowledge is objective and therefore not political
There is a political dimension but benefits outweigh risks
The original curriculum was also politically motivated but not recognised as such
Students themselves are demanding these changes

listening.title: The Philosophy of Expertise
listening.context: A philosopher discusses scepticism towards expert knowledge.

script.speaker: Host
script.text: We live in a period of deep scepticism towards expertise. Professor Crane, as a philosopher of knowledge, how do you make sense of this?

script.speaker: Professor
script.text: We must distinguish two phenomena being conflated under the label anti-expertise. One is a legitimate concern: how do we know who to trust when experts disagree, findings are sometimes reversed, and institutional interests sometimes corrupt expert opinion? That is reasonable. The other is less defensible: blanket rejection of expert consensus on empirically settled questions — vaccine safety, climate science — based on intuition or social media exposure. The first is critical thinking; the second is something closer to epistemic nihilism.

script.speaker: Host
script.text: Is there a way to cultivate the first without falling into the second?

script.speaker: Professor
script.text: Yes. It involves teaching the difference between uncertainty at the research frontier, where disagreement is the norm, and uncertainty at the level of established consensus, where disagreement is the exception. Most public misunderstanding conflates these two. When a new study challenges a previous finding, it is headline news. When the underlying consensus is stable, it is not a story. So the public receives a distorted picture: endless revision, endless doubt.

questions.question: What distinction does Professor Crane draw?
options: Between legitimate scepticism about who to trust versus rejecting established empirical consensus

questions.question: What does epistemic nihilism mean here?
options: A school valuing practical over theoretical knowledge
The view that no knowledge or truth claims can be relied upon
A form of academic scepticism applied to research

questions.question: Why does the professor say the public receives a distorted picture of science?
options: Media focuses on challenging new studies while ignoring stable consensus
Universities publish too much unreviewed research

listening.title: The Architecture of Cities
listening.context: A cultural critic discusses urban design as political expression.

script.speaker: Host
script.text: Your book argues that cities are ideological texts — that their physical form encodes political assumptions. Can you explain?

script.speaker: Critic
script.text: It is most visible in planning decisions that are no longer visible — because they have been naturalised as simply how cities are. Take the twentieth-century prioritisation of the car. The expansion of road networks, the destruction of urban rail infrastructure in the United States in the nineteen fifties, the design of suburbs that make walking impossible — these were not neutral technical decisions. They reflected specific assumptions about who the city was for, about private property versus public space, and about which forms of freedom mattered.

script.speaker: Host
script.text: And what alternative vision do you propose?

script.speaker: Critic
script.text: What urban theorists call the fifteen-minute city — the idea that all essential needs should be accessible within a fifteen-minute walk or cycle from any residence. It sounds modest but the implications are radical: a fundamental redistribution of commercial, educational, and cultural infrastructure away from centralised hubs towards distributed neighbourhood provision. The fierce resistance it encounters tells you a great deal about whose interests are embedded in the existing urban form.

questions.question: What is the critic's central argument about cities?
options: Urban planning has become too focused on aesthetics
The physical design of cities reflects and embeds specific political values
Modern cities are poorly designed compared to historical ones
Car-centred design results primarily from corporate lobbying

questions.question: What does the fifteen-minute city concept propose?
options: Reducing car journey times to fifteen minutes
Ensuring all essential services are within a fifteen-minute walk or cycle
Building new residential areas fifteen minutes from city centres
Limiting construction to districts near public transport

questions.question: What does the resistance to the fifteen-minute city reveal?
options: The existing urban form serves groups who benefit from the current arrangement
The concept is technically impractical in large cities

listening.title: Global Health Inequity
listening.context: A public health expert addresses a conference.

script.speaker: Expert
script.text: The COVID-19 pandemic exposed with uncomfortable clarity the degree to which global health outcomes are determined not primarily by biology but by geography, income, and political will. A child born in a high-income country has access to interventions — vaccination schedules, diagnostic infrastructure, specialist referral — that remain aspirational in much of the global south, not because the science is unknown but because the political economy of healthcare has never prioritised equitable delivery. The vaccine rollout of twenty twenty-one made this grotesquely visible: wealthy nations secured doses for multiples of their populations while healthcare workers in lower-income countries waited months. The intellectual property regime governing pharmaceutical innovation was designed to reward private investment. Whether it can be reformed to also serve public health goals — particularly given climate-related disease emergence — is one of the defining governance questions of the coming decades. What is not in question is whether the existing system is equitable. It is not, and we have the data to prove it.

questions.question: What is the expert's central argument?
options: Biological differences explain most variation in global health outcomes
Global health outcomes are primarily determined by socioeconomic and political factors
Vaccination rates are the single most important determinant of population health

questions.question: What specific example illustrates global health inequity?
options: Wealthy nations securing multiple doses per person while poorer countries waited
Failure of international organisations to coordinate
Higher vaccine hesitancy in lower-income countries

questions.question: What does grotesquely visible imply about inequity during the pandemic?
options: It revealed inequity in a way that was stark and impossible to ignore
The pandemic recorded inequity accurately for the first time
International media reported on it in an exaggerated way
Deaths in wealthy nations overshadowed problems elsewhere

speaking.instruction: Describe this image in detail and comment on what it suggests about a broader theme or issue.
speaking.assessmentNote:
📊 Assessment criteria:
• Lexical resource — use precise, advanced vocabulary
• Grammatical range — use complex structures (relative clauses, passive voice, conditionals)
• Coherence — organise your response logically; use linking words
• Analytical depth — go beyond basic description; share your interpretation

Aim for 50–70 seconds. Avoid short, simple sentences.

📊 Assessment criteria:
• Lexical resource — use precise, advanced vocabulary
• Grammatical range — use complex structures (relative clauses, passive voice, conditionals)
• Coherence — organise your response logically; use linking words
• Analytical depth — go beyond basic description; share your interpretation

speaking.instruction: Analyse this image. Describe it fully, interpret its meaning, and discuss what it communicates and how effectively.
speaking.assessmentNote:
📊 Assessment criteria:
• Sophisticated vocabulary — precise word choice, idiomatic language, nuanced expression
• Grammatical complexity — nominalisations, cleft sentences, inversion, mixed conditionals
• Extended coherent discourse — clear structure, well-developed argument with examples
• Critical thinking — analysis, evaluation, multiple perspectives

Aim for 80–100 seconds. Demonstrate the full range of your English — this is your opportunity to show C1 level.
